elements.sent-2411-001: Feynman's modification to electrodynamics is shown to be equivalent to a Hamiltonian approach developed by Podolsky.$^{ {\cite{PodolskySchwed }$}.}
elements.sent-2411-002: In the early days of QED, around 1930s, calculations of various electrodynamic processes were plagued by infinities.
elements.sent-2411-003: During the Shelter Island conference, in 1947, Kramers made the suggestion that certain infinities, such as the self-energy, could be absorbed into the mass of the electron.
elements.sent-2411-004: On a train ride from New York to Schenectady after the conference, Bethe did a nonrelativistic calculation of the 2S-2P level shift for hydrogen employing the mass renormalization idea suggested by Kramers.
elements.sent-2411-005: He got a logarithmic divergence for the shift because in the non-relativistic theory the electron self-energy is linearly divergent.
elements.sent-2411-006: He suspected that a relativistic treatment of the electron would yield a finite result for the level shift because the self-energy would only be logarithmically divergent.$^{ {\cite{Schweber }$ Returning to Cornell, after the Shelter Island conference, Bethe gave a lecture on the subject and suggested that if there were any modifications (relativistic) to electrodynamics that would render the self-energy calculation finite (even if the resulting theory were unphysical) then carrying out the mass renormalization and level-shift calculation would be easy and unambiguous.$^{ {\cite{FeynmanNobel }$ After Bethe's lecture Feynman approached Bethe and said he could do it.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: During his graduate years in Princeton Feynman had developed, in collaboration with his advisor John Wheeler, a version of electrodynamics called the ``Wheeler-Feynman absorber theory".
elements.sent-2411-008: He had studied in detail every which way to modify electrodynamics.$^{ {\cite{FeynmanNobel }$ Feynman's modification was to change the Dirac-delta $\delta$ appearing in the Fokker action \eqref{FokkerAction for classical electrodynamics by a narrow peaked function $f$.$^{ {\cite{FeynmanClassical }$ \begin{equation}\label{FokkerAction} S= \sum_{a} {m_{a} \int \sqrt{da_{\mu} da^{\mu}}} + \sum_{\underset{a,b}{a\neq b}}{ e_{a}e_{b} \int \int \delta(s^{2}_{ab}) da_{\mu}db^{\mu}}\end{equation} Feynman's modification to quantum mechanical perturbation calculation of self-energy was to first write the term $d\bm{k /k$ (appearing in the calculations) equivalently as $2\int \delta(\omega^2 - k^2) d\omega d\bm{k}$, where integration over $\omega$ is taken only for positive values, and then to replace the $\delta(\omega^2 - k^2)$ by \begin{equation}\label{FeynmanMod} \int_{0}^{\infty} \left( \delta(\omega^2 - k^2) - \delta(\omega^2 - k^2 - \lambda^2) \right) G(\lambda) d\lambda,\end{equation} where $G(\lambda)$ is some smooth function such that $\int_{0 ^{\infty G(\lambda)d\lambda = 1$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: This is equivalent to a choice of $f$ shown in Eq. \eqref{Ffunction .$^{ {\cite{FeynmanSpaceTime }$ Feynman applied this relativistic cut-off procedure to a free electron (spin-1/2 case) interacting with the electromagnetic field and showed that the self-energy of a free electron does indeed have the form of a relativistic mass correction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: This was shown in his 1948 Physical Review paper titled ``Relativistic Cut-Off for Quantum Electrodynamics".$^{ {\cite{FeynmanQuantum }$ Feynman's method of doing quantum mechanics was to start from an action integral, e.g. Fokker action \eqref{FokkerAction , and then using his ``path integral" approach get results, agreeing with standard quantum theory, directly without the gymnastics of the usual Hamiltonian approach.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The choice of $f$ shown in Eq. \eqref{Ffunction is equivalent, in the Hamiltonian scheme, to adding an extra term $-\frac{1 {2 a^{2 \frac{\partial F^{\mu\nu {\partial x^{\nu }\frac{\partial F_{\mu\gamma {\partial x_{\gamma }$ to the usual Lagrangian density of the electromagnetic field.$^{ {\cite{PodolskySchwed }$ This equivalence of the choice of $f$ shown in Eq. \eqref{Ffunction and the Hamiltonian scheme is shown in section \ref{FeynmanHamiltonian of this paper.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In 1948, Dyson had calculated the self-energy of a free electron while neglecting its spin (essentially a spin-0 case).$^{ {\cite{Dyson }$ This was published before Feynman's 1948 Physical Review paper, so the modification suggested by Feynman had not been applied for the spin-0 case.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The self-energy calculation for spin-0 case is algebraically simpler, compared to the spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ case, because of the absence of spin degree of freedom.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In section \ref{section3 of this paper, Feynman's modification \eqref{FeynmanMod is applied to the spin-0 case using the Hamiltonian scheme.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Carrying out the integrals \eqref{FeynmanSelfEnergy involved in the self-energy calculation of spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ case is rather tedious and involves various substitutions and integration by parts to reduce to elementary integrals.$^{ {\cite{FeynmanQuantum }$ In this paper, the terms obtained in the self-energy calculation of spin-0 case are reduced to similar terms from the spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ case.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus the relativistic cut-off procedure carried out by Feynman for spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ case can readily be applied to the spin-0 case as well.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Carrying out the self-energy calculation of a free electron using non-covariant perturbation method results in some terms that don't have the form of a relativistic mass correction.
elements.sent-2411-018: On account of the result being formally infinite, self-energy cannot be unambiguously mass renormalized using non-covariant ``old-fashioned perturbation" method.
elements.sent-2411-019: This struggle of the old-fashioned perturbation theory was remedied by the development of QED calculation techniques that made Lorentz invariance manifest throughout the calculations.
elements.sent-2411-020: In a theory free of infinities, calculation of self-energy of a free electron (even using the old-fashioned perturbation method) would result in terms that have the form of a relativistic mass correction, which could then be absorbed into the mass of the electron to carry out the mass renormalization procedure.
elements.section-2411-002: Feynman's modification in the Hamiltonian method
elements.sent-2411-021: During the 1940s several physicists were investigating different modifications to classical electrodynamics to solve the self-energy problem.
elements.sent-2411-022: Podolsky \& Schwed published$^{ {\cite{PodolskySchwed }$ one such modification where they simply added an extra term $-\frac{1 {2 a^{2 \frac{\partial F^{\mu\nu {\partial x^{\nu }\frac{\partial F_{\mu\gamma {\partial x_{\gamma }$ to the usual Lagrangian density and were able to get a modified electrodynamics that was free from infinities due to a point source.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this modified electrodynamics the four-vector potential $A_{\mu (x)$ satisfies the following relation\footnote{In Gaussian units. \begin{equation}\label{AvectorEqPodSch} a^{2}\Box^{2} A_{\mu}+ \Box A_{\mu}= 4\pi j_{\mu},\end{equation} where $\Box=\partial^{\mu}\partial_{\mu}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The usual relation is obtained by setting $a=0$.
elements.sent-2411-025: To carry out the perturbation calculation one needs the mode expansion of the free fields $A_{\mu}$ satisfying \begin{equation}\label{AvectorEqNoSource} a^{2}\Box^{2} A_{\mu}+ \Box A_{\mu}= 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-026: It can be shown that $A_{\mu = A^{(1) _{\mu - A^{(2) _{\mu}$, such that $\Box A^{(1) _{\mu = 0$ and $a^{2 \Box A^{(2) _{\mu + A^{(2) _{\mu = 0$, satisfies Eq. \eqref{AvectorEqNoSource .}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Thus the mode expansion of $A_{\mu}$ can be expressed as the difference between the mode expansions of the fields $A^{(1) _{\mu}$ and $A^{(2) _{\mu}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Feynman's modification \eqref{FeynmanMod effects the same correction with $G(\lambda)= \delta(\lambda - \lambda_{0 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Feynman's modification to electrodynamics was effected by replacing the Dirac-delta $\delta$ appearing in the Fokker action \eqref{FokkerAction by an appropriate function $f$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: A convenient choice for $f$ made by Feynman was\footnote[4]{This expression was given incorrectly in \cite{FeynmanQuantum but later corrected in \cite{FeynmanSpaceTime .. \begin{equation}\label{Ffunction} f= \frac{1}{2\pi^{2}}\int \left(\delta(k_{0}^2 - k^2)- \delta(k_{0}^2 - k^2 - \lambda^{2})\right)\sin(k_{0}|t|)\cos(\bm{k}\cdot \bm{x})\Theta(k_{0}) G(\lambda)\ dk_{0} d\bm{k}\ d\lambda.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{Ffunction} f= \frac{1}{2\pi^{2}}\int \left(\delta(k_{0}^2 - k^2)- \delta(k_{0}^2 - k^2 - \lambda^{2})\right)\sin(k_{0}|t|)\cos(k\cdot x)\Theta(k_{0}) G(\lambda)\ dk_{0} dk\ d\lambda.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-031: The four-vector potential $A_{\mu (x)$ due to particle ``$b$" (defined originally with a $\delta$ function) becomes \begin{equation}\label{modifiedA} A_{\mu}(x) = \int f(s_{xb}^{2}) db_{\mu},\end{equation} where $s_{xb ^{2 =(x-b)_{\mu (x-b)^{\mu}$ and $b_{\mu}$ are the coordinates of $b$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: This is same as Eq. \eqref{AvectorEqPodSch with $a=1/\lambda_{0}$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Feynman's modification can thus be carried out in the Hamiltonian scheme as well.
elements.sent-2411-035: The self-energy $(\Delta E_{p}\footnote[5]{The self-energy is more accurately given by $\Delta E_{p = \int_{0 ^{\infty}\left[ \Delta E_{p (\lambda) - \Delta E_{p (0)\right]\, G(\lambda) \, d\lambda.$ $^{ {\cite{FeynmanQuantum }$})$ of a free electron (spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ case) with momentum $\bm{p}$ and bare mass $m$ that Feynman$^{ {\cite{FeynmanQuantum }$ obtained was \begin{equation}\label{FeynmanSelfEnergy} \left(2\pi^2/e^{2} \right)E_{p} \Delta E_{p}= \big(m^{2}+ \frac{1}{2}\lambda^{2}\big) \int \frac{1}{(E_{f} + \omega)^{2} - E_{p}^{2}} \cdot \frac{E_{f}+ \omega}{E_{f}\ \omega}d\bm{k} + \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d\bm{k}}{\omega}- \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d\bm{k}}{E_{f}},\end{equation} where $E_{p =\sqrt{\bm{p ^{2 + m^{2 }$, $\omega=\sqrt{k^{2 + \lambda^{2 }$, and $E_{f =\sqrt{(\bm{p + \bm{k )^{2 + m^{2 }$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The correction to mass that Feynman obtained as a result was \begin{equation}\Delta m = \frac{m\, e^{2}}{\pi}\left( \frac{3}{2}\ln \left(\frac{\lambda_{0}}{m} \right)+ \frac{3}{2}\right) \quad \text{where,} \quad \ln \lambda_{0} = \int_{0}^{\infty} G(\lambda)\ln \lambda\, d\lambda.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-037: In the limit as $\lambda_{0 \rightarrow \infty$, one recovers the result of usual electrodynamics where the mass correction diverges logarithmically.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The mass correction term given above is also Lorentz invariant.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, the mass correction obtained from usual electrodynamics by employing the old-fashioned perturbation calculation will result in a term that is not Lorentz invariant.$^{ {\cite{Schweber }$ Since formally the result diverges, the Lorentz non-invariant term wouldn't matter.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, if mass renormalization is to be performed unambiguously then the result, although formally divergent, should be Lorentz invariant.
elements.sent-2411-041: QED calculation techniques later developed by Feynman, Tomonaga, Schwinger, and Dyson do exactly this: the perturbation calculations are done in a manner that makes the Lorentz invariance apparent at every stage of the calculation.
elements.sent-2411-042: The corresponding expression for the self-energy of a spin-0 particle with momentum $\bm{p}$ and bare mass $m$ is \begin{equation}\label{Spin0SelfEnergy} \left(2\pi^2/e^{2} \right)E_{p} \Delta E_{p}= \big(m^{2}- \frac{1}{4}\lambda^{2}\big) \int \frac{1}{(E_{f} + \omega)^{2} - E_{p}^{2}} \cdot \frac{E_{f}+ \omega}{E_{f}\ \omega}d\bm{k} + \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d\bm{k}}{\omega}+ \frac{1}{4} \int \frac{d\bm{k}}{E_{f}}\end{equation} There are two difficulties that must be overcome when carrying out self-energy calculation for the spin-0 case in the Hamiltonian method.
elements.sent-2411-043: The usual difficulty of conjugate momentum vanishing also persists in the modified electrodynamics.$^{ {\cite{PodolskySchwed }$ Podolsky \& Schwed modified the Lagrangian so that conjugate momenta don't vanish but nonetheless lead to the same equations of motion for the fields.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The other difficulty is using a gauge condition that is consistent with the commutation relations of the fields.
elements.sent-2411-045: The approach used in this paper follows the theory of constraints outlined by Dirac in his book ``Lectures on Quantum Mechanics".$^{ {\cite{Dirac }$ Even with this approach, certain gauge choices, for example the Coulomb gauge $\nabla \cdot \bm{A = 0$, cannot be used.}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Following Galvão \& Pimentel$^{ {\cite{CarlosPimentel }$ a ``generalized Coulomb gauge", written as $(1-a^{2}\Box)\nabla \cdot \bm{A = 0$, is instead used as the gauge condition on the fields.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: With this choice of gauge, application of the theory of constraints leads to the following modified commutation relations for the fields $A_{\mu ^{(1)}$ and $A_{\mu ^{(2)}$ and their conjugate momenta: \begin{equation}[A_{i}^{(1)}(x), P_{j}^{(1)}(y)] = i \hbar\, \delta_{ij} \delta^{3}(x-y) [A_{i}^{(2)}(x), P_{j}^{(2)}(y)] = -i \hbar\, \delta_{ij} \delta^{3}(x-y) - i \hbar\, \partial_{i}\partial_{j} G\end{equation} where, $\mathcal{G}$ satisfies the partial differential equation $(1-a^{2}\nabla^{2 )\nabla^{2 \mathcal{G = \delta^{3 (\bm{x -\bm{y )$ and $i,j=1,2,3$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The implication of these modified commutation relations is that, for the part $\bm{A ^{(1)}$ of the field everything is the same as it is for the usual (unmodified) electromagnetic field $\bm{A}$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: However, for the part $\bm{A ^{(2)}$ things become different.}
elements.sent-2411-050:
First, the commutation relations of the creation and annihilation operators of this field become  egin{equation}[\hat{a}^{(2)}_{\lambda}( m{p}), \hat{a}^{\dagger (2)}_{
u}( m{q})] = -\delta_{\lambda 
u} \delta^{3}( m{p}- m{q})\end{equation} for the polarizations $\lambda, 
u = 1, 2$ perpendicular to $ m{p}$, and  egin{equation}[\hat{a}^{(2)}( m{p}), \hat{a}^{\dagger (2)}( m{q})] = - rac{m^{2}}{E_{p}^{2}}\delta^{3}( m{p}- m{q})\end{equation} for the polarization along the direction of $ m{p}$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Second, the negative sign in the commutation relation means that the norm of some states will be negative.
elements.sent-2411-052: This is not desirable for a physical theory; it results in negative probabilities, which is nonsensical.
elements.sent-2411-053: In order to avoid this difficulty, one must define the action of the operators $\hat{a ^{(2) _{\lambda (\bm{p )$ and $\hat{a ^{\dagger (2) _{\lambda (\bm{p )$ on the state vectors $|n, \lambda \rangle$ ($ |n, \lambda \rangle$ are eigenstates of the number operator $N_{\lambda =-\hat{a ^{\dagger (2) _{\lambda}\hat{a ^{(2) _{\lambda}$) such that: \begin{equation}\hat{a}^{(2)}_{\lambda}| n,\lambda \rangle = -\sqrt{n}\, |n-1\rangle \quad and \hat{a}^{\dagger(2)}_{\lambda}| n,\lambda \rangle = \sqrt{n+1}\, |n+1\rangle,\end{equation} and also redefine the inner product as $\langle n |\mathcal{O | n \rangle$ so that the inner product comes out positive for all states $| n \rangle$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Here, $\mathcal{O}$ is an operator that has the following properties:.
elements.sent-2411-055: This way of avoiding the difficulty of negative norm was first carried out by Gupta$^{ {\cite{Gupta }$ and independently by Bleuler in 1950.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: After sorting through these difficulties and applying of the ``old fashioned perturbation" method one obtains the self-energy expression \eqref{Spin0SelfEnergy for a spin-0 particle interacting with the electromagnetic field.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Thus, Feynman's modification \eqref{FeynmanMod (or equivalently the modification suggested by Podolsky \& Schwed in the Hamiltonian scheme) for the spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ case applies almost without any change to the spin-0 case as well.}}
